candidates.case_name: HIGH FASHION NEW MEDIA CORPORATION LTD (suing on behalf of itself and also in its capacity as the sole shareholder of LONGFORD INFORMATION AND TECHNOLOGY CO LTD) v. LEONG MA LI
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Chu VP and G Lam JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A person subject to a court injunction can be held liable for contempt if their agent breaches the order, provided the agent was acting within the scope of their authority. The principal cannot avoid liability by denying knowledge or claiming willful ignorance of the agent's actions.

For a finding of vicarious liability for contempt based on an agency relationship, the specific allegations regarding the nature of the agency, the scope of authority, and the principal's control must be properly pleaded to allow for a fair inquiry and presentation of evidence.

A key factor in establishing an agency relationship for the purpose of attributing liability is the principal's ability to exercise control over the agent's actions concerning the subject matter of the injunction.

principles.principle_label: Fiduciary duty not automatic with company seal
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no automatic principle that an employee who receives a company seal from a company officer for company purposes thereby owes fiduciary duties to that individual officer personally.

principles.principle_label: Legal relations distinct from corporate officer
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a bank account is held in a company's name, the bank's legal relationship is with the company, not the individual officer. The use of an officer's personal seal on company documents does not necessarily create or affect a legal relationship between that officer and the bank.

relationships.target_case_name: Hone v Page
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the principle stated by Slade J regarding when a person bound by an injunction is liable for an agent's breach.

relationships.target_case_name: Crown Times International Ltd v Chan Yim Ping & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment notes that this Hong Kong case adopted the principle from Hone v Page as correct.

relationships.target_case_name: China Metal Recycling (Holdings) Limited (in compulsory liquidation) v Chun Hei Man & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment notes that this Hong Kong case also adopted the principle from Hone v Page as correct.

candidates.case_name: ALLIED WELI DEVELOPMENT LTD (In Liquidation) v. CHUANG YUE-CHIEN EUGENE AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge KC Chan in Chambers (Paper Disposal)

principles.principle_label: Directors' fiduciary duties to creditors in insolvency
principles.paraphrase_en: When a company is at or near insolvency, directors owe fiduciary duties to consider the interests of creditors, and implementing transactions that deplete company assets for no consideration to the prejudice of creditors constitutes a breach of those duties.

In complex, high-value transactions, the complete absence of relevant documentation from a company and the inability of examined persons to produce it or recall details can support an adverse inference against the parties responsible.

A complaint that a pleaded case of conspiracy defrauds pleading principles and that judicial reasoning is inapplicable to demurrable pleas of fraudulent conspiracy, when based on a selective application of such principles, has no reasonable prospect of success on appeal.

principles.principle_label: Appeal prospect for shadow directorship plea
principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal against a refusal to strike out a plea of shadow directorship has no reasonable prospect of success.

relationships.target_case_name: Leung Chin Sing, Rabo v Ko Chun Hay, Kelvin
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case's observation regarding adverse inferences from missing documentation in complex transactions.

candidates.case_name: INVEST GAIN LTD v. NOVEL GOOD LTD
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Proper notice requirement for contractual claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A party's claim under a contract may fail if proper or effective notice as required by the contract is not given, regardless of the underlying merits of the claim.
The court must interpret contractual clauses that purport to confine or limit a party's liability according to their proper construction and effect.
A court may consider the merits of a claim even after finding a procedural defect (like improper notice) that disposes of the claim, particularly where the matter may be subject to appeal.

candidates.case_name: LAM KAI KWONG v. UP CREATION LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Leung in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Expert evidence admissibility - relevance to issues
principles.paraphrase_en: Expert evidence is only admissible if it addresses specific questions of liability or causation that the court must determine, and parties should focus their expert evidence on those relevant disciplines.

Questions regarding the condition, quality, or probability of events based on degrees of factual circumstances are matters for the court to determine based on witness evidence and pleaded cases, not for expert opinion.
An employer's liability for workplace accidents may depend on the provision of appropriate protective equipment, which is a factual dispute to be determined by the court based on evidence.

candidates.case_name: CHAN KI v. GENERAL SECURITY (H.K.) LTD
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge Levy in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: WONG YUN WA, administratrix of the estate of CHAU CHUK KAN v. SURPLUS LINK LTD trading as CHOI FOOK DELIGHT BANQUET AND CHOI FOOK LUXURY BANQUET
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Lawrence KF Ng in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For an injury to be considered 'caused by accident' under the relevant legislation, the claimant must prove that a distinct event or series of events caused the injury. The injury itself cannot be considered the accident; there must be an external causative event separate from the injury.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for accident causation
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal burden rests on the claimant to demonstrate, on a balance of probabilities, that a specific event or series of events was the cause of the personal injury suffered.

principles.principle_label: Rejection of internal failure as accident
principles.paraphrase_en: An unexpected internal physiological failure, such as a cardiac arrest or stroke, does not in itself constitute an 'accident' for the purposes of compensation legislation, absent proof of an external causative event.

When assessing whether work conditions (like high temperature or physical exertion) triggered an injury such as a stroke, the court must examine medical evidence to determine if those factors caused or substantially contributed to the onset of the injury.

relationships.target_case_name: LKK Trans Ltd v Wong Hoi Chung
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the interpretation of section 5(1) of the relevant ordinance.

relationships.target_case_name: Faulds (likely referring to Chief Adjudication Officer v Faulds)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles established in Faulds regarding the definition of 'personal injury caused by accident'.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary of State for Work and Pensions v Scullion
relationships.description: The judgment notes that the UK Court of Appeal applied the Faulds principle in this case.

relationships.target_case_name: Chiu Kwai Yuk v Ascent Exhibition Design (Hong Kong) Limited & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment finds the observations in this case helpful when considering the cause of a stroke.

relationships.target_case_name: Stewart v Wilsons and Clyde Coal Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment notes that older UK authorities like this one were rejected in later cases as treating 'accident' too broadly.

candidates.case_name: POOSAALA RAM PRASAD v. THE HONGKONG AND SHANGHAI BANKING CORPORATION LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: When considering an application to strike out a claim, the court must assume all facts pleaded by the plaintiff to be true for the purpose of that application.
Questions that depend on the resolution of disputed facts or the assessment of evidence reliability are matters to be determined at trial, not at an interlocutory stage.
The reliability or unreliability of expert evidence is an issue to be evaluated and determined during the trial, not at pre-trial interlocutory stages.

candidates.case_name: CHINA CANDY HOLDINGS LTD v. HLB HODGSON IMPEY CHENG LTD
judges: Before: Hon Wilson Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Damage to listing status as asset
principles.paraphrase_en: A company's listing status constitutes a valuable asset (a chose in action), and exposure to a risk of delisting causes an immediate diminution in its value, which is actionable damage.

A purely contingent liability does not constitute actionable damage until the contingency occurs, whereas a 'damaged asset' situation involves immediate damage when a possible future liability renders an asset less valuable.
A legal principle established in a case concerning one type of loss (e.g., trading losses) is not applicable to a claim involving a fundamentally different type of loss or damage.

relationships.target_case_name: Law Society v Sephton
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Sephton that a purely contingent liability does not constitute damage until the contingency occurs.

relationships.target_case_name: Wardley Australia Ltd v State of Western Australia
relationships.description: The judgment cites Wardley Australia Ltd as the authority applied by the House of Lords in the Sephton case regarding contingent liabilities.

relationships.target_case_name: Shore v Sedgwick Financial Services Ltd & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites Shore as an example of the 'damaged asset' situation where actual damage occurs when an asset is rendered less valuable.

relationships.target_case_name: Re China Energy Holdings Ltd (No 2)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the holding from this case that a company's listing status is a valuable asset as a chose in action.

relationships.target_case_name: China Shanshui Cement Group Ltd v Mi Jingtian
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that the possibility of delisting constitutes damage to a company's listing status asset.

candidates.case_name: HE ZHIJUAN v. HONG YIP SERVICE COMPANY LTD
judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Phillis Loh (by Paper Disposal)

candidates.case_name: THE JOINT AND SEVERAL LIQUIDATORS OF DAYS IMPEX LTD (IN LIQUIDATION) v. MAHESH NANIK DAYARAM
judges: Before: Hon Harris J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Section 48(1) of the relevant ordinance can apply to claims brought under section 275.
Section 275 grants the court a broad discretionary power to order payment of any sum it considers appropriate, which may be more or less than the original debt amount.
Orders under insolvency provisions are motivated by the policy objective of increasing funds available for distribution to creditors, which aligns with the overall purpose of the insolvency regime.

relationships.target_case_name: Cyona Distributors
relationships.description: The judgment cites Russell LJ's holding in Cyona Distributors regarding the compensatory nature of damages and debt remedies.

candidates.case_name: INVEST GAIN LTD v. NOVEL GOOD LTD
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The interpretation of contractual clauses requiring written notice of a warranty claim depends entirely on the specific wording of each individual clause; there is no single governing principle beyond examining the particular language used.

The primary approach to interpreting a contractual clause is to consider the natural and ordinary meaning of its language as the best guide, before considering commercial implications or rules about construing ambiguous clauses narrowly.

If linguistic, contextual, and purposive analysis does not clearly resolve an ambiguity in an exclusion clause, the court may prefer the narrower construction, but this is not a presumption or a rule allowing strained meanings; commercial parties are free to allocate risks as they choose.
A contractual clause requiring notice of a claim by a specified deadline can operate as a time bar and exclusion clause, excluding liability if notice is not given in compliance with its terms.

principles.principle_label: Sufficiency of notice for warranty claims
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether a bald notice of a claim (without detailed particulars) is sufficient to satisfy a contractual notification provision depends on the specific wording and context of the clause, including any related provisions that may affect its operation.

relationships.target_case_name: ROK plc (in Administration) v S Harrison Group Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that clauses requiring written notice of warranty claims are common in share purchase agreements and take many forms.

relationships.target_case_name: RWE Nukem Limited v AEA Technology plc
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the principle that every notification clause must be interpreted based on its own individual wording.

relationships.target_case_name: Forrest v Glasser
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that each notice clause must be construed according to its own terms and discusses its analysis of a bald notice being sufficient under a specific clause.

relationships.target_case_name: Nobahar-Cookson v The Hut Group Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies principles from this case regarding the interpretation of exclusion clauses, including using natural meaning as the starting point and preferring narrower constructions only if ambiguity remains after full analysis.

candidates.case_name: KAMALJIT KAUR v. FAIRWOOD FAST FOOD LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge James Kwan in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Test for conditional leave to defend
principles.paraphrase_en: The test for granting conditional leave to defend is whether the defence or counterclaim raised can be considered shadowy or lacking in substance.

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of trial witness statements in interlocutory applications
principles.paraphrase_en: Witness statements filed for the main trial are generally inadmissible for use in an interlocutory application for interim payment.

principles.principle_label: Accident arising out of employment requirement
principles.paraphrase_en: For a work injury claim, the accident must arise out of the employment, not merely occur in the course of employment; an attack on an employee as a person in the street, not specially targeted due to employment circumstances, does not satisfy this requirement.

principles.principle_label: Presumption and evidence to the contrary in employment injury cases
principles.paraphrase_en: If there is evidence that the accident did not arise out of or in the course of employment, the statutory presumption does not apply, and the applicant bears the ordinary burden of proving their case.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof in disputed factual applications
principles.paraphrase_en: Where facts are disputed in an application and cannot be resolved without a trial, the applicant must prove their case on the evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Asparouh Ianev Dimitrov v Dominic Tak Ming Lau
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case for the principle that the test for conditional leave to defend is whether the defence is shadowy.

relationships.target_case_name: Hollywood Palace Co Ltd v Trans-Global Hong Kong Investment Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case indirectly as the authority for the shadowy defence test referenced in Dimitrov.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Yuet Yung
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this Court of Appeal case where a claim was dismissed for failure to prove personal injury by accident arising out of and in the course of employment.

candidates.case_name: HUANG WEIHONG v. TAO KEE ENGINEERING COMPANY LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Leung J in Chambers

candidates.case_name: LAW SIN HA v. CONFIDENTIAL MATERIALS DESTRUCTION SERVICE LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Charles Wong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: While surveillance footage generally has limited value in assessing the genuineness of psychiatric impairments, it may be relevant where it captures specific behaviors that contradict the claimant's reported symptoms or medical records.

principles.principle_label: Causation for compensation under Employees' Compensation Ordinance
principles.paraphrase_en: For an injured employee to receive compensation for temporary incapacity, it is a precondition that the incapacity results from the injury. The statutory presumption relates to the period of necessary absence, not the cause, so the employee must still establish causation.

principles.principle_label: Treatment compliance as evidence of genuine symptoms
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant's consistent engagement with prescribed treatments (such as medication, therapy, and social work follow-ups) over an extended period can support the genuineness of their symptoms and is not merely an act to advance a claim.

principles.principle_label: Temporal and symptomatic connection for psychiatric injury
principles.paraphrase_en: The development of psychiatric symptoms shortly after an accident, where the symptoms are directly connected to the accident (e.g., nightmares, flashbacks, avoidance of the scene), can support a finding that the injury resulted from the accident, especially when there is no pre-existing condition.

Discretionary bonuses or incentives may be considered part of an employee's earnings under the Employees' Compensation Ordinance if they are of a recurring nature, as evidenced by patterns in salary records, and are linked to the employer's performance and the employee's work.

relationships.target_case_name: Ale Thak Prasad v Tsang Chin Keung & Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that symptoms of PTSD are largely subjective and treating doctors often accept the patient's reported symptoms without verification.

relationships.target_case_name: Tse Lai Sing v Tung Wah Group of Hospitals
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case alongside Ale Thak Prasad regarding the subjective nature of PTSD symptoms and medical reliance on patient reports.

relationships.target_case_name: Lai Yuen Hing v Lo Chi Hung & Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case, as referenced in Hong Kong Civil Procedure, for the principle that an injured employee must establish causation between injury and incapacity for compensation.

relationships.target_case_name: LKK Trans Ltd v Wong Hoi Chung
relationships.description: The judgment references this case in a comparative discussion about the statutory presumption relating to the period of absence under the Employees' Compensation Ordinance.

candidates.case_name: THE CHINESE CULTURAL STUDIES ASSOCIATION formerly known as THE CHINESE CULTURAL STUDIES ASSOCIATION LTD v. CHEUNG LAI NGAN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge C. K. Siu in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff may only apply for summary judgment under Order 14 rule 1 of the Rules of the District Court if the defendant has first given notice of intention to defend the action.

candidates.case_name: BARCO INVESTMENTS LTD v. WONG YAN HO (also known as GEOFFREY WONG YAN HO) AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's power to amend an order under the slip rule is limited to correcting errors in expressing the court's manifest intention, not to altering the substance of the decision.
The court retains an inherent power to clarify its original order after it has been sealed, provided the court's intention from the judgment is clear and no prejudice is caused to the other party.

principles.principle_label: Discretionary nature of slip rule relief
principles.paraphrase_en: Granting relief under the slip rule is discretionary, and the court may refuse it if intervening circumstances make rectification inexpedient or inequitable.

relationships.target_case_name: Lo Yuen Chong v IWS Environmental Technologies Limited
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the summary of principles regarding applications under Order 20 rule 11 set out in that case.

relationships.target_case_name: Man Ping Nam & another v Man Fong Hang (No. 2)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the court's inherent power to clarify its original order after sealing.

relationships.target_case_name: MKKWH v RKSH
relationships.description: The judgment applies the discretionary principle from this case that slip rule relief may be refused if rectification would be inexpedient or inequitable.

candidates.case_name: WAH SUN INVESTMENTS LTD v. WEALTHY BASE PROPERTIES LTD
judges: Before: Madam Recorder Sit, SC in Chambers

candidates.case_name: GLORY SKY FINANCE LTD v. CHEN JIANHUA AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Suen SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A taxing master must not issue a final certificate of taxation before the period for applying for a review of the taxation decision has expired.

When considering an application to extend time for a review of taxation, the court should consider: (1) the length of the delay; (2) the reasons for the delay; (3) the merits of the proposed review; and (4) the prejudice to the other party.
Good reasons to re-open a taxation under Order 62 rule 17B exist where a party's failure to object or attend the hearing was not due to their fault or was caused by factors beyond their control.

principles.principle_label: Exclusion of carelessness as good reason
principles.paraphrase_en: Carelessness, oversight, or pre-occupation do not constitute good reasons for granting an extension of time or re-opening a matter.

relationships.target_case_name: Hong Kong Civil Procedure 2022
relationships.description: The judgment cites the commentary in Hong Kong Civil Procedure 2022 to support the principle that carelessness does not constitute a good reason for extension.

candidates.case_name: SANTA MONICA EQUITY INC. v. CHRISTELLE FILA NZOLOLO
judges: Before: Hon Anthony Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Committal for forgery in civil proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may entertain an application for committal based on alleged forgery of a document adduced in related civil proceedings, such as an application to set aside a statutory demand.

Where a respondent has been properly served with all relevant documents pursuant to a court order for substituted service but chooses not to participate, the court may order the hearing to proceed in their absence.

principles.principle_label: Evidential burden for setting aside statutory demand
principles.paraphrase_en: A party applying to set aside a statutory demand based on an alleged debt owed to them must adduce evidence in support of that debt; a mere assertion or a single document of questionable authenticity may be insufficient.
A party may commission a report from handwriting experts to examine the authenticity of a document and its signature when its genuineness is in doubt, and such a report may be admissible as evidence.

candidates.case_name: LANTAU INTERNATIONAL SCHOOL LTD v. SECRETARY FOR EDUCATION
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A public authority's policy must not be applied so rigidly that it prevents the proper consideration of individual circumstances, thereby unlawfully fettering the decision-maker's discretion.
An authority is entitled to operate a general policy to govern its approach to applications, provided it still considers the individual merits of each case before applying that policy.

principles.principle_label: Distinction between policy and its application
principles.paraphrase_en: A challenge to the lawfulness of a policy itself is distinct from a challenge to its application; a policy may be lawful in principle but become unlawful if implemented too rigidly in practice.

relationships.target_case_name: Collymore
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Collymore that a general policy is permissible but must not be implemented so rigidly as to prevent proper individual consideration.

relationships.target_case_name: Adath Yisroel
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Adath Yisroel that a policy must not be so rigid as to amount to an unlawful fetter on discretion.

candidates.case_name: H&M HENNES & MAURITZ GBC AB v. ZOETOP BUSINESS CO LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Lok J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish joint liability as a joint tortfeasor, three criteria must be satisfied, as established by the leading authority on the subject.

relationships.target_case_name: Sea Shepherd UK v Fish & Fish Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment treats this UK Supreme Court case as the leading authority on the legal test for joint tortfeasorship.

candidates.case_name: BRIGHT SHIPPING LTD v. CHANGHONG GROUP (HK) LTD
judges: Before: Hon Anthony Chan J in Chambers

relationships.target_case_name: Fortenova Grupa v LLC Shushary Holding
relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Fortenova Grupa on the basis that the redemption in that case was indisputably unlawful, unlike the present situation.

relationships.target_case_name: Celestial Aviation Services Ltd v Unicredit Bank AG
relationships.description: The judgment cites Celestial Aviation Services as a related authority on the topic of payments involving sanctioned entities.

candidates.case_name: TOP GEAR INVESTMENT LTD AND OTHERS v. PACM GROUP (HOLDINGS) LTD
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The court is bound to follow the established legal principle regarding what constitutes a penalty clause in a contract.

principles.principle_label: No obligation prior to definitive agreement
principles.paraphrase_en: Where parties are negotiating towards a loan, there is no binding obligation on either party to enter into the loan agreement until definitive loan documents are executed.

principles.principle_label: Implied term for best endeavours not established
principles.paraphrase_en: An argument for implying a term requiring parties to use their best endeavours to complete a transaction, based on business efficacy, was raised but not substantively developed.

An argument hinting at the application of the prevention principle—that a party should not deliberately prevent the occurrence of a condition precedent—was referenced but not fully developed in submissions.

relationships.target_case_name: Kensland Realty Ltd v Whale View Investment Ltd & another
relationships.description: The judgment notes that counsel's arguments hint at an application of the prevention principle established in this case.

candidates.case_name: WONG KEAVEN KA HAY t/a HOI SHING SEAFOOD CO. v. CENTRAL INTERNATIONAL (HK) LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Eugene Yim in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: SUNNI INTERNATIONAL LTD (IN LIQUIDATION) v. KAO WAI HO FRANCIS AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: For a serious allegation like unlawful means conspiracy, the court must find the inference of concerted action and shared unlawful intent to be compelling and plainly established based on the primary facts.

To establish unlawful means conspiracy, the claimant must prove the defendant was sufficiently aware of the surrounding circumstances and shared the same unlawful object (intention to injure) with other conspirators.
A party may, for practical reasons, abandon part of its claim for relief even when it is entitled to that relief in principle, thereby limiting the scope of the judgment.

candidates.case_name: LAI CHEE YING v. TA KUNG PAO (HONG KONG) LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The modern judicial trend is to exercise discretion to order jury trials only rarely, reflecting a gradual development in practice.

Whether the proviso in section 33A(1) of the High Court Ordinance applies depends on the unique circumstances of each case, considering if the dispute turns on a broad picture rather than minute details.

relationships.target_case_name: International Social Service (Hong Kong Branch) v Vision First Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the proposition that there is a modern trend of rarely exercising discretion to order jury trials.

relationships.target_case_name: Ho Kwan Yiu
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from this case regarding how to determine if the s.33A(1) proviso applies based on whether issues depend on a broad picture or small details.

candidates.case_name: CHINA CONCRETE COMPANY LTD v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The court possesses jurisdiction to grant a declaration that certain conduct is not criminal, either regarding actual/threatened proceedings or future conduct, but this jurisdiction should only be exercised in truly exceptional circumstances.

A defendant in properly instituted criminal proceedings should not generally be permitted to seek a civil declaration to pre-empt or 'hive-off' the construction of a criminal statute from the criminal court, as this constitutes an impermissible intrusion into the criminal court's domain.

An allegation of fraud or dishonesty must be pleaded with sufficient particularity; particulars of facts that are merely consistent with honesty are insufficient, and the defendant must know the primary facts from which dishonesty is to be inferred.

principles.principle_label: Private law right enforcement involving public law
principles.paraphrase_en: A litigant possessing a private law right may enforce it through an ordinary action, even if the proceedings incidentally involve examination of a public law act or decision.

A plea of specific intent to injure requires facts from which such intent can be inferred; it is insufficient if the pleaded facts do not negate a genuine and honest belief in the lawfulness of the defendant's position.

relationships.target_case_name: Imperial Tobacco Ltd v Attorney General
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle from Imperial Tobacco that it is generally impermissible for a civil court to grant a declaration on criminality after criminal proceedings have commenced.

relationships.target_case_name: Roy v Kensington and Chelsea and Westminster Family Practitioner Committee
relationships.description: The judgment cites Roy for the principle that a private law right can be enforced by ordinary action even if it involves incidental examination of a public law decision.

candidates.case_name: WELL BORN REAL ESTATE MANAGEMENT LTD v. LEE TAK LUN ANDREW (Also known as Andrew Lee)
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Harold Leong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining the meaning of a statement, the relevant context is what a reasonable person, considering all circumstances, would understand to be the context.
The court must determine the ordinary and natural meaning of allegedly defamatory words by examining them within their proper context.
A defence of qualified privilege is defeated if the defendant did not hold a subjective honest belief in the truth of the published statement, which constitutes malice.
For a defence of qualified privilege to succeed, the defendant must have subjectively held an honest belief in the truth of the statement at the time of publication.
The defence of qualified privilege requires that the defendant's motive in publishing the statement was to further a legitimate common interest, not to knowingly publish untruths.

candidates.case_name: WOON THEAN MUN v. HONG KONG AIRLINES LTD
judges: Before: Hon Marlene Ng J in Court

principles.principle_label: New points on appeal - fairness principle
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court should generally refuse to entertain a new legal point not raised in the first instance proceedings, based on the foundational imperative of fairness to prevent unfair surprise to the respondent.

New points on appeal that are fact-sensitive or would have affected the course of evidence or conduct of the case at the hearing below should not be allowed, even if they do not strictly necessitate further evidence.

The Flywin principle restricting new points on appeal, though originating in civil trial contexts, is a principle of general application that also applies to appeals against tribunal awards or determinations on points of law.

It is generally not open for an appellant on appeal against a tribunal determination to take a point that was not pleaded in the proceedings below, even though tribunal hearings are conducted informally.
On appeal, the court should not allow the appeal merely on grounds of misdirection or improper admission/rejection of evidence unless substantial wrong or miscarriage of justice has been occasioned.

relationships.target_case_name: Flywin Co Ltd v Strong & Associates Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Flywin doctrine as a discretionary principle preventing new points on appeal based on fairness considerations.

relationships.target_case_name: Timothy Wynn Owen KC & anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support that the Flywin principle is one of general application beyond civil trials.

relationships.target_case_name: Nice & Well Ltd v Fu Mee Yuk Shirley
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority that points not pleaded below are generally not open to an appellant on appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheng Loon Yin v Secretary for Justice & anor
relationships.description: The judgment references this case in discussing the limitations on raising new points on appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: Ladd v Marshall
relationships.description: The judgment references the Ladd v Marshall requirements as relevant to the admission of fresh evidence on appeal.

candidates.case_name: CONVOY COLLATERAL LTD v. CHO KWAI CHEE (also known as CHO KWAI CHEE ROY) AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: HONG KONG FAR INFRARED RAYS ASSOCIATION LTD v. LAM HONG NAM AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Court

candidates.case_name: SHENZHEN HONEYCOMB SYSTEM CO LTD v. HCT TECHNOLOGIES (HONG KONG) CO LTD
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Indemnity costs may be ordered against a party who pursues an appeal that lacks reasonable prospects of success, particularly where the appeal appears to be a delaying tactic against the enforcement of an arbitral award.

A court may refuse to stay the enforcement of an arbitral award pending an appeal if it finds the appeal has no reasonable prospect of success and if enforcement would be prejudiced by delay, especially where the award creditor is a foreign entity.
A procedural challenge to the authority of a party to institute enforcement proceedings for an arbitral award can be dismissed with costs if the challenge lacks merit.

relationships.description: The judgment references the prior decisions of Mimmie Chan J and Anthony Chan J in the same proceedings without expressing disagreement.

candidates.case_name: SIR ELLY KADOORIE & SONS LTD (FOR AND ON BEHALF OF ITSELF, ITS CURRENT AND FORMER OFFICERS, EMPLOYEES AND AGENTS, INCLUDING ITS LEGAL REPRESENTATIVES, MESSRS SIMMONS & SIMMONS) v. SAMANTHA JANE BRADLEY
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge H. Au-Yeung in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Order 14A suitability - legal vs factual issues
principles.paraphrase_en: The Order 14A procedure for determining questions of law or document construction is inappropriate when factual issues are interwoven with the legal issues, as the court cannot decide legal questions based on assumed or hypothetical facts and would need to hear evidence to resolve factual disputes.

principles.principle_label: Order 14A scope - final determination of claims/issues
principles.paraphrase_en: Order 14A can be invoked not only to finally determine the entire cause or matter, but also to finally determine any specific claim or issue within it, subject to the court's discretion.

The question of law or document construction to be determined under Order 14A must be stated in clear, careful, and precise terms without difficulty, obscurity, or ambiguity regarding what is to be decided.
The court's discretion under Order 14A is wide, but the procedure is not intended for the determination of trivial matters, even if the formal requirements for its use are satisfied.

A challenge to a plaintiff's locus standi (standing to sue) must be raised at the outset of proceedings or when it comes to the attention of the court or defense, and must be decided promptly; it is inappropriate to raise it by way of defense and allow the action to proceed without deciding the standing issue.

relationships.target_case_name: Allen v Gulf Oil Refining Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Allen v Gulf Oil Refining Ltd as authority for the requirement that questions for determination under Order 14A must be formulated clearly and precisely.

relationships.target_case_name: Shell Hong Kong Ltd v Yeung Wai Man Kiu Yip Co Ltd & Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that the Order 14A procedure should not be used for trivial matters, despite the court's wide discretion.

relationships.target_case_name: 佛山市宏達發展公司清算組 v East Legend Investment Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this Court of Appeal case regarding the proper timing and necessity for deciding challenges to a plaintiff's locus standi.

relationships.target_case_name: Korso Finance Establishment Anstalt v. John Wedge
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported Court of Appeal case as an example where specific factors were relevant to exercising discretion to allow a question to be determined under Order 14A.

candidates.case_name: EXXON LTD AND ANOTHER v. ZENG CHENG ENGINEERING COMPANY LTD
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge G. Chow in Chambers (open to public)

principles.principle_label: No general right to dispose of uncollected goods
principles.paraphrase_en: At common law, a bailee does not have a general right to dispose of goods that the bailor has refused or is unable to collect.

An involuntary bailee who performs an act that would normally constitute conversion is only liable if they acted without reasonable care. Acts done in good faith with the objective of returning goods to the owner are considered reasonable.
Placing or leaving a chattel on another's land without consent constitutes trespass. The landowner is entitled to remove the offending chattel, provided they take reasonable care in doing so.

principles.principle_label: Recovery of removal costs in trespass
principles.paraphrase_en: In cases of trespass to land, the costs of removing offending articles or illegal structures are recoverable as damages from the trespasser.

relationships.target_case_name: Sachs v Miklos
relationships.description: The judgment references this case for the proposition that a bailee might imply consent to a sale by giving warning, but notes the difficulties with this approach.

relationships.target_case_name: Desir Anthony C v Knight Frank (Services) Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this local case regarding the modified liability standard for involuntary bailees acting in good faith.

relationships.target_case_name: Jones v Gospel & White
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle established in this English Court of Appeal decision that a landowner may remove trespassing chattels with reasonable care.

candidates.case_name: STANDARD CHARTERED BANK (HONG KONG) LTD v. LOK SHEK HUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Teresa Wu in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A person who signs a legal document is generally bound by its terms, even if they did not read or understand the details before signing.

principles.principle_label: No general equity jurisdiction for improvident bargains
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no general equitable jurisdiction to set aside transactions merely due to inequality of bargaining power or because a bargain proves to be improvident or harsh for one party.

principles.principle_label: Limited scope of unconscionability under UCO
principles.paraphrase_en: The Unconscionable Contracts Ordinance (UCO) targets unconscionable contracts, not all unconscionable conduct, and assessment is confined to the contract's terms at the time of making, specifically in consumer dealings.

principles.principle_label: Guidance from foreign jurisprudence on unconscionability
principles.paraphrase_en: Foreign jurisprudence (e.g., Australian law) on similar statutory concepts like 'unconscionable conduct' may provide guidance in interpreting the UCO, but its broader scope must be distinguished from the Hong Kong ordinance's narrower focus.

relationships.target_case_name: Bank of China (Hong Kong) Ltd v Fung Chin Kan & Another
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle established in this case that a signatory is bound by a document's terms even if not read.

relationships.target_case_name: Ming Shiu Chung
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that unconscionability is not a general remedy for adjusting harsh contracts between competent parties.

relationships.target_case_name: Chang Pui Yin v Bank of Singapore Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the approach from this case that Australian jurisprudence on unconscionable conduct can offer guidance for interpreting the UCO.

candidates.case_name: LINE POWER LTD v. CHEN JINHUI
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Maria Yuen in Chambers

candidates.case_name: SAM FAI CONSTRUCTION CO LTD v. YAT MING CONSTRUCTION & ENGINEERING LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers

candidates.case_name: ZPMC OFFSHORE SERVICE CO LTD (Formerly known as SHANGHAI ZHENHUA HEAVY INDUSTRIES SHIPPING CO LTD) v. PHILIP JEFFREY ADKINS AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Harris J in Court

principles.principle_label: Statutory exception to Foss v Harbottle rule
principles.paraphrase_en: Sections 728-730 of the Companies Ordinance (Cap. 622) create a statutory exception to the rule in Foss v Harbottle, allowing members to seek remedies for conduct affecting the company under specified circumstances.

A member's right to commence an action under section 729 is limited to situations where their interests 'have been, are or would be affected' in their capacity as a member, not merely because a wrong has been done to the company.

principles.principle_label: Relationship between member and company actions
principles.paraphrase_en: If a company is willing and able to take action to remedy a wrong done to it, a member's interests are unlikely to be impacted in a way that engages section 729, avoiding conflict and duplication of proceedings.

Division 3 of Part 14 of the Companies Ordinance provides remedies for members regarding others' conduct in relation to companies, including injunctions, declarations that contracts are void or voidable, and damages.

relationships.target_case_name: Foss v Harbottle
relationships.description: The judgment explains that Division 3 of Part 14 of the Companies Ordinance creates a statutory exception to the rule in Foss v Harbottle.

candidates.case_name: KOK WAI LEUNG v. HO TIN BUILDING MATERIAL LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Leung in Court

candidates.case_name: ZHANG QIANG v. CISCO SYSTEMS (HK) LTD
judges: Before: Hon K Yeung in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The determination of whether a legally binding contract has been formed is based on an objective assessment of the parties' exchanges, not on their subjective intentions. Agreement on material terms (consensus ad idem) is required.

At common law, for a contract to be void due to unilateral mistake, the non-mistaken party must have actual knowledge of the other party's mistake regarding a material term. Constructive knowledge alone is insufficient for the common law principle.

Equity provides a separate, flexible jurisdiction to grant relief (such as rendering a contract voidable) in cases of unilateral mistake where the non-mistaken party has constructive knowledge and there is evidence of unconscionable conduct or sharp practice.
The legal concept of severance does not apply in the context of a contract affected by unilateral mistake.

When assessing a witness's credibility, the court must consider the inherent probability of their evidence, its consistency with undisputed evidence and contemporaneous documents/conduct, and its internal consistency. Demeanor is relevant but undue weight should not be placed on it.

relationships.target_case_name: Shanghai Tongji
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Final Appeal's explanation of the objective test for determining contract formation.

relationships.target_case_name: Shogun Finance Ltd v Hudson
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Phillips' explanation of the objective approach to contract formation and the qualification regarding consensus ad idem.

relationships.target_case_name: Ching Chi Sau v Yip Woon Yin Judy
relationships.description: The judgment accepts a submission based on this case regarding the approach to witness credibility.

relationships.target_case_name: Chwee Kin Keong v Digilandmall.com Pte Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Singapore case as an example of a jurisdiction drawing a distinction between actual knowledge for voidness and a wider equitable jurisdiction for voidability.

candidates.case_name: YAO GUOLIANG AND ANOTHER v. FOREVER WINNER INTERNTIONAL LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.principle_label: Nature of shares as legal choses in action
principles.paraphrase_en: Shares in a company constitute legal choses in action, not chattels. Share certificates are physical chattels that evidence ownership, but the rights and obligations of shareholders are governed by the company's articles of association and the authorized terms of issue.

principles.principle_label: Transfer of legal title to chattels
principles.paraphrase_en: Transferring legal title to chattels requires an act equivalent to delivery to the transferee, coupled with the transferor's intention to transfer ownership. The requisite intention is usually clear in a sale contract, but for a gift, it must be proven, potentially aided by evidential presumptions depending on the relationship between the parties.
Whether a breach is technical is assessed by reference to the nature of the obligation breached and the consequences of that breach.

The seriousness of alleged contempt and the utility of contempt proceedings are fact-dependent. The significance of any particular factor varies according to the specific context and facts of the case.

relationships.target_case_name: Navigator Equities Limited, Vladimir Anatolevich Chernukhin v Oleg Vladimirovich Deripaska
relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority for the principle that determining if a breach is technical involves examining the obligation's nature and the breach's consequences.

candidates.case_name: GOLDJET DEVELOPMENT LTD v. RANDAL ALAN WHITECOTTON AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge To in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court assesses witness credibility by considering factors such as consistency, demeanor, corroboration, potential interest in the outcome, and the witness's general character and respect for the law.

Evidence from a witness with no stake in the litigation and no affiliation with the parties can provide valuable corroboration for the evidence of other witnesses, though its weight may depend on the witness's access to full information.

The court must exercise caution and give little weight to the evidence of a witness who is found to be smart and selective in telling the truth, unless that evidence is corroborated by other reliable evidence.

A witness's credibility is severely undermined by a blatant contradiction between their current testimony and a prior sworn statement, casting doubt on their willingness to testify truthfully according to their interests.

In assessing a late application, the court should consider the prejudice caused by distracting the other party from preparing for trial, which is a recognized form of prejudice, particularly grave if the trial has already commenced.

relationships.target_case_name: Chun Wo Building Construction Ltd v Metta Resources Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that prejudice from a late application includes distracting the other party from trial preparation.

candidates.case_name: GOLDJET DEVELOPMENT LTD v. RANDAL ALAN WHITECOTTON AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge To in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Amendment of pleadings - necessity and prejudice
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may allow amendments to pleadings if they are necessary to determine the real question in controversy between the parties, the application is made promptly, and the opposing party would not suffer prejudice as a result.

principles.principle_label: Indemnity costs - litigation conduct and sanctioned offers
principles.paraphrase_en: A party may be awarded indemnity costs based on the opposing party's litigation conduct and for failing to accept a sanctioned offer, pursuant to the Rules of the High Court.
A court may award interest on costs under the Rules of the High Court where a sanctioned offer is not accepted.

Reserved costs, if any, should be awarded to the successful party in the relevant proceedings and taxed on a party and party basis, unless the court has specific knowledge justifying a different basis.

candidates.case_name: MTM LAB JAPAN LTD AND OTHERS v. ABACUS LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: His Honour Judge MK Liu in Chambers (Open to Public) (Remote Hearing)

principles.principle_label: Threshold for serious question to be tried
principles.paraphrase_en: For an interlocutory application, the plaintiff only needs to demonstrate that there is a serious question to be tried regarding the defendant's involvement in the alleged acts, which is not a very high threshold to meet.

principles.principle_label: No common law tort of harassment
principles.paraphrase_en: Hong Kong common law does not recognize a standalone tort of harassment.

relationships.target_case_name: Pong Seong Teresa & Ors v Chan Norman
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the proposition that there is no tort of harassment at common law in Hong Kong.

candidates.case_name: CITYLINKERS GROUP LTD v. CENTURY CROWN INVESTMENT LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Grace Chow in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Whether a party is a money-lender is a factual inquiry specific to each case, with no fixed definition or formula. The court must examine the evidence to see if the lender falls within the statutory definition or has advertised/held themselves out as a money-lender.

principles.principle_label: Approach to summary relief under MLO s.23
principles.paraphrase_en: Summary relief regarding a statutory discretion (such as under s.23 of the Money Lenders Ordinance) may only be granted where no reasonable court would refuse to exercise that discretion.

principles.principle_label: Need for full factual picture for discretion
principles.paraphrase_en: The court requires a full picture of all circumstances to be reasonably confident at an interlocutory stage that no reasonable court would refuse to exercise a statutory discretion under the Money Lenders Ordinance.

principles.principle_label: Judicial reluctance to relieve non-compliant lenders
principles.paraphrase_en: Courts should be slow to grant relief to money lenders who fail to comply with the regulatory Ordinance, as doing otherwise would frustrate the legislative purpose, especially given potentially inadequate criminal sanctions.

principles.principle_label: Contextual limitation of factors for reopening loans
principles.paraphrase_en: Factors considered relevant when exercising discretion under one section of the Money Lenders Ordinance (e.g., s.25) may not be directly applicable to the exercise of discretion under a different section (e.g., s.23), particularly where the statutory framework has changed.

relationships.target_case_name: Conroy v Kenny
relationships.description: The judgment applies the approach from Conroy v Kenny for determining if a lender is a money-lender, including the two-stage evidential analysis.

relationships.target_case_name: Vinson Finance Limited v Chu Qingzhu & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites Vinson Finance for the principle that summary relief may be granted where no reasonable court would refuse to exercise a statutory discretion.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheung Chow v Cheung Ng Sheong Steven
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Appeal's reasoning in Cheung Chow that a full factual picture is needed before concluding no reasonable court would refuse to exercise a discretion.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Kwai Fun v Li Fung
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes Wong Kwai Fun by noting its reasoning was specific to the discretion under s.25 of the MLO and the statutory context was different.

candidates.case_name: YANG YUEGUANG v. DRAGON HILL DEVELOPMENT LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: The Hon Mr Justice S T Poon in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Strike-out test under RHC O.18 r.19
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may strike out pleadings under RHC Order 18 rule 19(1)(a), (b), and (d) and its inherent jurisdiction if they disclose no reasonable cause of action, are scandalous, frivolous, or vexatious, or are otherwise an abuse of the court's process.
The limitation period for recovering a loan is six years from the date the cause of action accrues, as prescribed by section 4(1)(a) of the Limitation Ordinance (Cap. 347).

principles.principle_label: Commencement of limitation period for loans
principles.paraphrase_en: For a claim to recover a loan, the limitation period begins to run from the date the loan was made or from the date repayment was due, not from the date of any subsequent acknowledgment or agreement.

candidates.case_name: NU LOOK IPI ENTERPRISES LTD v. CHAN SIU MUI and TAM LAN CHI LORCHE, the Administratrix of the estate of TAM KAM FAI FRANCIS, deceased
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Paul Lam SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A part payment of a debt operates as an acknowledgment of the debt, which can restart the limitation period for recovery.
For a part payment to restart the limitation period, the payment must be made by the debtor or by an agent acting with the debtor's authority.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Footman Bower & Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Buckley J's holding regarding the legal effect of part payments on limitation periods.

relationships.target_case_name: Lee Kwok Wing v Chung Chuen Hei
relationships.description: The judgment references the holding of DHCJ Lisa Wong SC on the principle of part payment as acknowledgment.

relationships.target_case_name: Chinnery v Evans
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes this case as involving payment by an agent of the liable party, which is not applicable to the present facts.

candidates.case_name: ALPHA & LEADER ASSOCIATES LTD v. VICTORY MEGA TRADING LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Winnie Tsui in Chambers

candidates.case_name: TRENUS OCEANWAY LTD v. RICHLAND INTERNATIONAL LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Harris J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Expert opinion evidence is inadmissible if it purports to answer factual questions that are reserved for the judge's determination, rather than explaining specialized knowledge or practices relevant to the subject matter.

Expert evidence is permissible and potentially helpful when it explains specialized practices or standards (such as industry-specific accounting practices) that are outside the ordinary knowledge of the court.

candidates.case_name: TECHTRONIC PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT LTD AND ANOTHER v. FAUTEUX, DENIS GASTON
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge MK Liu in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal to the Court of Appeal is not a de novo hearing or retrial; the appellant must identify a justifiable basis for appellate intervention in the lower court's decision.

principles.principle_label: Limits on raising new points on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Appeal has discretion to entertain new points on appeal, but it is firmly established that fact-sensitive new points, or those affecting the course of evidence or conduct of the case below, should generally not be allowed, especially if they could lead to counter-arguments requiring fresh consideration.
A party is generally bound by a concession made by their counsel at a prior hearing, even if they are later represented by a new legal team.

principles.principle_label: Test for release from implied undertaking
principles.paraphrase_en: The overarching test for releasing a party from the implied undertaking not to use disclosed documents for collateral purposes is whether release would achieve justice on the facts, requiring cogent persuasive reasons and special circumstances to justify the grant.

principles.principle_label: Status of prosecution concessions in precedent
principles.paraphrase_en: A concession offered by the prosecution in a previous case does not constitute a binding ruling by the court, especially where the court expressly refrained from commenting on its correctness, and thus provides no authoritative assistance.

relationships.target_case_name: SJ v Cheng Ka Yee
relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes SJ v Cheng Ka Yee by noting that a point relied upon was merely a prosecution concession, not a ruling, and the CFA refrained from endorsing it.

relationships.target_case_name: Jewish Federation of Greater Washington Inc v Aiwo Trading Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case in the context of the required demonstration of cogent and persuasive reasons for release from an implied undertaking.

candidates.case_name: CHINA CONSTRUCTION BANK (ASIA) CORPORATION LTD v. KLARENKEN ENTERPRISE LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon K Yeung J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Discharge of debt by placing funds at creditor's disposal
principles.paraphrase_en: A debtor's payment obligation is discharged when securities or funds held by the creditor are, by the debtor's instruction, placed at the creditor's disposal for settlement, regardless of whether the creditor actually applies them.

principles.principle_label: Condition for funds being at creditor's disposal
principles.paraphrase_en: For funds to be considered at a creditor's disposal, the creditor must have the practical ability to apply them. Funds subject to litigation or rival claims that prevent the creditor from accessing them are not at the creditor's disposal.

relationships.target_case_name: Trustor AB
relationships.description: The judgment cites Trustor AB to illustrate the principle that funds are not at a creditor's disposal if litigation prevents their access.

candidates.case_name: THE JOINT AND SEVERAL LIQUIDATORS OF GREEN ELITE LTD (IN OFFICIAL LIQUIDATION IN THE BRITISH VIRGIN ISLANDS) v. STANDARD CHARTERED BANK (HONG KONG) LTD
judges: Before: Hon Fung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: For a court to find a director liable under section 120(1) of the BVI Business Companies Act, it must be satisfied that the director acted dishonestly. A director who holds a genuine but mistaken belief about their entitlement to deal with company assets is not dishonest.
A director can be held personally liable for a loan that appears in a company's accounts if that director alone is responsible for the transaction.

principles.principle_label: Requirements for pre-action disclosure against third parties
principles.paraphrase_en: To obtain pre-action disclosure from a third party, the applicant must show: (1) a real prospect the information may lead to locating or preserving assets subject to a proprietary claim; (2) the request for documents must be as specific as a subpoena; and (3) the court must balance the potential advantage of disclosure against the detriment to the person from whom disclosure is sought.
A party is estopped from reopening a factual issue that has been conclusively determined by a court of competent jurisdiction in prior proceedings.

principles.principle_label: No need for discovery where destination of funds is known
principles.paraphrase_en: Discovery or disclosure from a third party (such as a bank) is not necessary to realize assets if it is already known where the funds in question have gone, and the claimant can trace the funds directly to the recipients.

relationships.target_case_name: Nam Tai Property Inc v IsZo Capital LP & anr
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that dishonesty is required under section 120(1) of the BVI Business Companies Act.

candidates.case_name: ALPHA & LEADER ASSOCIATES LTD v. VICTORY MEGA TRADING LTD
judges: Before : Hon Kwan VP and Cheung JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Duty of full and frank disclosure
principles.paraphrase_en: The duty to make full and frank disclosure applies to ex parte applications, but not to inter partes hearings where the other party has had a fair opportunity to present evidence.

For a failure to disclose to be material, it must relate to a matter that the court was required to consider in granting the relief sought. If the duty of disclosure does not arise, the alleged non-disclosure is not a ground for setting aside the order.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of evidence in interlocutory applications
principles.paraphrase_en: In an interlocutory application, the court does not make definitive findings of fact; its role is to assess whether there is a good arguable case based on the evidence presented.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of explanations for lack of evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering a party's lack of evidence to refute allegations from separate proceedings, the court should take into account any reasonable explanation provided for that lack of evidence, such as a settlement made on a no-admission basis.

relationships.target_case_name: Shogun Finance Ltd v Hudson
relationships.description: The present judgment holds that the trial judge properly addressed the defendant's argument based on this case regarding the identity of parties to the contract.

candidates.case_name: LUI KWONG SHING trading as WAI SING ENGINEERING COMPANY v. PINE WOOD (F.E.) INTERNATIONAL LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Matthew Ho in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for contractual conditions
principles.paraphrase_en: A party alleging breach of a contractual condition must provide evidence to support that allegation; the absence of contemporaneous complaint when a breach should have been obvious can support a finding that the condition was satisfied.

The court may reject a witness's evidence as exaggerated or inconsistent when it is contradicted by documentary records and the witness's claimed attitude is at odds with their demonstrated conduct in correspondence.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for factual findings
principles.paraphrase_en: Factual disputes in civil cases are resolved on the balance of probabilities, with the court preferring one party's evidence over another's where conflicts exist.
When interpreting a signed undertaking, the court examines its actual terms to determine its legal effect, not a party's subjective belief about its purpose, unless induced by misrepresentation.

principles.principle_label: Inference from failure to call material witness
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a prima facie case is established and a party fails to call a material witness who could speak to the relevant facts without plausible explanation, the court may draw an adverse inference against that party.

candidates.case_name: SMARTEK E & M ENGINEERING (HK) LTD v. WIN WIN WAY CONSTRUCTION CO LTD
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Andrew Li in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A witness's evidence may be rejected if it contradicts undisputed contemporaneous documents, defies common sense, and is inconsistent with common industry practice.

principles.principle_label: Formation of partly written, partly oral contracts
principles.paraphrase_en: A contract may be formed partly in writing and partly orally, where an unsigned written document can form the foundational framework of the agreement, supplemented by subsequent oral discussions.
Evidence from a witness who no longer works for a party may be considered particularly convincing as they can be viewed as independent.

principles.principle_label: Accrual of cause of action for goods/services
principles.paraphrase_en: The cause of action for the supply of goods or services generally arises when the goods are delivered or services are supplied, absent special contractual terms to the contrary.

principles.principle_label: Procedural payment terms and cause of action
principles.paraphrase_en: Procedural contractual provisions for payment assessment or demands do not delay the accrual of the cause of action for supplied goods or services.

relationships.target_case_name: China Light & Power Company Ltd v See Kong Silk Ltd & Ors
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that procedural payment terms do not affect when a cause of action accrues.

candidates.case_name: CHAPMAN INTERNATIONAL MACAO COMMERCIAL OFFSHORE LTD v. HEALTH LIFE TEXTILE (HK) COMPANY LTD
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge G. Chow in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for payment to third party
principles.paraphrase_en: When a debtor pays a third party instead of the creditor, the debtor bears the legal risk. If the money does not reach the creditor, the debtor must prove the creditor authorized the third party to receive payment, held the third party out as authorized, induced the debtor to believe so, or that a relevant trade custom existed.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for factual assertions
principles.paraphrase_en: A party asserting a fact (like an agreement) must establish it on the balance of probabilities. If they fail to raise even a prima facie case, the court cannot draw an adverse inference from the opposing party's failure to call a witness to refute it.

The court assesses a witness's credibility by examining the internal consistency of their evidence and their performance under cross-examination. Minor errors or exaggerations that are not material to the core issues do not necessarily destroy a witness's credibility.

The court makes findings of fact based on the evidence and the balance of probabilities. If a party fails to provide credible or satisfactory explanations for their assertions, the court will find they have not discharged their burden of proof.

relationships.target_case_name: Sky King Machinery Engineering Ltd v China Harbor Engineering Company Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that a failure to raise a prima facie case precludes drawing an adverse inference from the opponent's failure to call a witness.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Kim Hong v Chan Yan Hong & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case, though the specific principle for which it is cited is not detailed in the provided paragraphs.

candidates.case_name: MAMMY PANCAKE COMPANY LTD v. CARLA DAY LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Fung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Claims of fraud or dishonesty must be pleaded with sufficient detail; stating facts that could also indicate honest conduct is inadequate.
Where a party deliberately evades service, such as by returning properly sent mail to a provided address, the court may deem service to have been effectively completed.

candidates.case_name: TANG YUAN YI v. TOKYO INTERNATIONAL INVESTMENT LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge William Wong SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The fraud exception to summary judgment is not engaged merely because a claim arises from a background of fraud. It is only engaged if the plaintiff must allege and prove fraud against the defendant to establish or maintain its claim.

A court should not liberally infer an allegation of deliberate dishonesty where none is expressly made. An act or omission should only be considered inherently dishonest if its very nature implies dishonesty.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of all materials for fraud exception
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining if the fraud exception applies, the court must consider all relevant materials, including subsequent pleadings and affidavits in reply, not just the initial claim.

principles.principle_label: Unjust enrichment claim independent of fraud
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim in unjust enrichment against a recipient of funds can be established without the plaintiff needing to allege or prove fraud against that defendant, even if the funds originated from a scam perpetrated on the plaintiff by a third party.

relationships.target_case_name: Universal Capital Bank
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the test from Universal Capital Bank to determine whether the fraud exception to summary judgment is engaged.

relationships.target_case_name: Zimmer
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles established in Zimmer regarding the fraud exception in the context of an unjust enrichment claim.

relationships.target_case_name: Laerdal Medical Limited v Hong Kong Haocheng International Trade Limited
relationships.description: The present case follows the reasoning in Laerdal, where the fraud exception was held not to be engaged in a similar email fraud and unjust enrichment scenario.

relationships.target_case_name: Menfond Electronic Art & Computer Design Co., Ltd v Wong Wang Tat Victor & Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites Menfond for the principle that an allegation of deliberate dishonesty should not be inferred too liberally by the court.

candidates.case_name: WELLSON SILK TRADING LTD v. CHEUNG WAI YING AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A statutory derivative action is commenced by a shareholder applying for leave to bring proceedings in the company's name, which must be granted by the court before the writ is issued.

A minority shareholder may bring an action in the name of the company, despite the general rule that the company itself is the proper plaintiff, if leave is obtained from the court under the statutory derivative action procedure.
The court may grant leave to commence a statutory derivative action by consent of the parties, based on the affirmations filed in support of or in opposition to the leave application.

candidates.case_name: KWOK HIU KWAN v. CONVOY GLOBAL HOLDINGS LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.principle_label: Director's duty of good faith - subjective test with objective elements
principles.paraphrase_en: A director's duty to act in good faith in the company's best interests is generally subjective, but becomes more difficult to prove if the action caused substantial detriment to the company. If there is no evidence the director actually considered the company's interests, the test becomes objective: whether an intelligent and honest director could have reasonably believed the action was in the company's interests.

The duty to exercise powers for proper purposes is assessed objectively. The court must identify the power, its proper purpose, the actual purpose for which it was exercised, and determine if that purpose was proper. The director's honest belief about acting in the company's interests is irrelevant to this assessment.

principles.principle_label: No conflict duty - strict objective test
principles.paraphrase_en: The duty to avoid conflicts of interest is objective and strictly enforced. The test is whether a reasonable person would see a real or substantial possibility of conflict. This inflexible rule applies regardless of conscious wrongdoing or whether the company actually suffered loss.

principles.principle_label: Articles void if contrary to companies legislation
principles.paraphrase_en: Any provision in a company's articles of association that conflicts with the relevant companies legislation is void. This principle is based on public policy and applies generally.

principles.principle_label: Court's reluctance to micromanage internal affairs
principles.paraphrase_en: The court will generally not intervene to micromanage issues of internal company management that fall within the remit of the board of directors or shareholders in general meeting.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Peveril Gold Mines
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as part of the line of authority establishing that articles conflicting with companies legislation are void.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Greater Beijing Region Expressways Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Hong Kong case as part of the development of the principle that articles contrary to companies legislation are void.

relationships.target_case_name: Ghossoub v Team Y&R Holdings Hong Kong Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported Court of Appeal case which references and summarizes the principle from Re Greater Beijing Region Expressways Ltd.

candidates.case_name: NEW SPARKLE ROLL INTERNATIONAL GROUP LTD v. ZHENG HAO JIANG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Alexander Stock SC in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Subjective test for director's breach of duty
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a breach of duty by directors, the company must prove the directors acted dishonestly and not in the company's bona fide interests. The test is subjective, focusing on whether the directors honestly believed their actions were in the company's interest, not whether the actions were objectively beneficial or merely negligent.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for serious allegations
principles.paraphrase_en: The standard of proof for serious allegations such as fraud or dishonesty is commensurate with the gravity of the allegations. Inferences of dishonesty are not to be drawn lightly.

principles.principle_label: Caution in summary determination of dishonesty
principles.paraphrase_en: While it is permissible in principle to resolve allegations of dishonesty on a summary basis, the authorities indicate that courts should exercise caution in doing so.

candidates.case_name: RICH ENGINEERING LTD v. FIRE SERVICE INSTALLATION CONTRACTORS DISCIPLINARY BOARD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A disciplinary board must provide reasons for its sentencing decisions, including its choice of penalty, the determination of sentence length, decisions on concurrency or consecutiveness, and the application of the totality principle.

An appeal under Order 55 rule 3(1) is conducted as a rehearing, but the court must give proper weight to the original tribunal's decision and does not typically rehear evidence or permit re-cross-examination. The appellant must demonstrate the tribunal erred in law, departed from a principle, or misapprehended the facts.
An applicant alleging that a disciplinary board considered itself handicapped in its sentencing deliberations bears a heavy burden of proof to establish that fact.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: An applicant alleging that a disciplinary board considered itself handicapped in its sentencing deliberations bears a heavy burden of proof to establish that fact.

candidates.case_name: CLARK QUANTUM KENT (formerly known as John Mohammad Clark and John Julius Tasca) v. HAI TIN LTD (formerly known as Allied Finance Asia LTd) AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Barma JA and Au JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Test for leave to serve out of jurisdiction
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's approach to deciding whether to grant leave to serve process outside the jurisdiction is analogous to the test for summary judgment under Order 14 of the Rules of the High Court.

Summary judgment under Order 14 is generally not available in cases involving allegations of fraud, and the court should not investigate the merits of such allegations when determining if a good arguable case exists for service out of jurisdiction.
The court must be satisfied that the plaintiff has established a good arguable case on the merits when considering an application for leave to serve out of jurisdiction.
A plaintiff's claim must fall within one or more of the specified gateways under Order 11 of the Rules of the High Court to justify service of process outside the jurisdiction.

One available gateway for service out of jurisdiction under Order 11 is where the claim is brought to enforce, rescind, dissolve, annul or otherwise affect a contract, or to recover damages for breach of contract.

candidates.case_name: ORIENTAL TEXTILE PRODUCTS LTD v. ASIA TELEVISION HOLDINGS LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may still make a costs order against a party as a matter of principle, even if the opposing party indicates it does not seek costs, and it is for the recipient of the costs order to decide whether to enforce it.

candidates.case_name: KWOK LAI SHEUNG v. MORE INSURANCE BROKER COMPANY LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge B. Mak in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: CHINA PING AN INSURANCE OVERSEAS (HOLDINGS) LTD v. LUCK GAIN LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Jonathan Chang SC in Chambers
